---
title: "IE 48B — Homework 1"
subtitle: "Exploring Time Series through Time and Frequency Domains"
author: "Yunus Emre Camızcı | Student ID: 2021402078"
date: "Due: March 23, 2026, 23:59"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    number-sections: true
    theme: cosmo
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
    embed-resources: true
    self-contained: true
    fig-width: 9
    fig-height: 4
    fig-dpi: 150
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
---

---

::: {.callout-important}
## Dataset Assignment

Each student is assigned a **unique** dataset from the
[UCR Time Series Classification Archive](https://www.timeseriesclassification.com/).
Find your student ID in the table below to identify your dataset.
All analysis must be performed on **your assigned dataset** — submissions using a
different dataset receive zero credit.

| Student ID | Dataset |
|:----------:|---------|
| 2019\*\*\*078 | Rock |
| 2020\*\*\*162 | Beef |
| 2020\*\*\*180 | ShapeletSim |
| 2020\*\*\*240 | Ham |
| 2021\*\*\*015 | Wafer |
| 2021\*\*\*051 | Herring |
| 2021\*\*\*054 | FaceFour |
| 2021\*\*\*069 | GunPoint |
| 2021\*\*\*078 | Coffee |
| 2021\*\*\*153 | ECGFiveDays |
| 2021\*\*\*162 | Trace |
| 2021\*\*\*174 | OSULeaf |
| 2021\*\*\*216 | SyntheticControl |
| 2021\*\*\*264 | MedicalImages |
| 2021\*\*\*273 | Plane |
| 2021\*\*\*309 | Strawberry |
| 2022\*\*\*249 | ECG200 |
| 2022\*\*\*270 | CBF |
| 2023\*\*\*219 | ItalyPowerDemand |
| 2023\*\*\*255 | TwoLeadECG |
| 2024\*\*\*003 | Wine |
| 2024\*\*\*066 | SonyAIBORobotSurface1 |
| 2024\*\*\*279 | Lightning2 |
| 2024\*\*\*294 | BeetleFly |
| 2025\*\*\*069 | FordA |
| 2025\*\*\*081 | Fish |

Dataset details (classes, train/test sizes, domain) are in the homework PDF.
:::


> **Reproducibility requirement:** Set your random seed using your student ID
> in every code block that uses randomisation (e.g. `set.seed(XXXXXXXX)` in R
> or `np.random.seed(XXXXXXXX % 2**31)` in Python). Results that cannot be
> reproduced with this seed receive zero credit for the affected question.

> **Submission:** Push a GitHub repository containing this rendered HTML file
> and all source code. Repository structure must follow the course template
> (see syllabus). Add the instructor and TA as collaborators.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Work incrementally — commit after each part

After completing each part, render the document and push your progress:

```bash
quarto render HW1_TimeDomainFrequency.qmd
git add HW1_TimeDomainFrequency.qmd HW1_TimeDomainFrequency.html
git commit -m "Complete Part 2: missing data simulation"
git push
```

**Your commit history is assessed.** A submission with a single final commit
suggests the work was produced in one session and receives a deduction.
Aim for at least one commit per part.
:::

---

# Dataset Overview

**Assigned dataset:** *Coffee*  
**Number of classes:** *2*  
**Training set size:** *28*  
**Test set size:** *28*  
**Series length:** *286*  
**Domain / description:** *Time series representing spectroscopic measurements of coffee beans used for classification.*

Load your dataset and confirm the dimensions match the archive description.

```r
# R — example using tsclassification helper or direct download
# Adjust path to wherever you stored the .ts or .arff file

# library(tsrepr)        # or read manually
# Recommended: download the .ts format from the archive,
# then parse with readr / tsclassification package

# YOUR CODE HERE

# Expected output:
# dim(train_data)  →  [n_train x (length + 1)]   last column = class label
# dim(test_data)   →  [n_test  x (length + 1)]
```

```{python}
# Python — example using aeon or sktime
# pip install aeon  (or: pip install sktime)

# from aeon.datasets import load_classification
# X_train, y_train = load_classification("YourDataset", split="train")
# X_test,  y_test  = load_classification("YourDataset", split="test")

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from aeon.datasets import load_from_ts_file

STUDENT_ID = 2021402078

train_path = "data/Coffee_TRAIN.ts"
test_path = "data/Coffee_TEST.ts"

X_train, y_train = load_from_ts_file(train_path)
X_test, y_test = load_from_ts_file(test_path)

if X_train.ndim == 3:
    X_train = X_train[:, 0, :]
if X_test.ndim == 3:
    X_test = X_test[:, 0, :]

print("X_train shape:", X_train.shape)
print("y_train shape:", y_train.shape)
print("X_test shape:", X_test.shape)
print("y_test shape:", y_test.shape)

# Expected output:
# X_train.shape  →  (n_train, 1, length)   for univariate
# y_train.shape  →  (n_train,)
```

---

# Part 1 — Time Domain Exploration (25 pts)

## Representative plots

Plot **3 randomly selected time series per class**, arranged in a grid (classes in rows,
samples in columns). Label axes, include class labels in panel titles, and use consistent
y-axis limits across panels so amplitudes are comparable.

*Deliverable: one figure, one paragraph interpretation (≤ 100 words).*

```{python}
# set.seed(YOUR_STUDENT_ID)
np.random.seed(STUDENT_ID % 2**31)

classes = np.unique(y_train)
selected_indices = {}

for cls in classes:
    cls_idx = np.where(y_train == cls)[0]
    selected_indices[cls] = np.random.choice(cls_idx, size=3, replace=False)

all_values = []
for cls in classes:
    for idx in selected_indices[cls]:
        all_values.append(X_train[idx])

all_values = np.concatenate(all_values)
y_min, y_max = all_values.min(), all_values.max()

# Plot
n_rows = len(classes)
n_cols = 3

fig, axes = plt.subplots(n_rows, n_cols, figsize=(12, 4 * n_rows), squeeze=False)

for row, cls in enumerate(classes):
    for col, idx in enumerate(selected_indices[cls]):
        ax = axes[row, col]
        ax.plot(X_train[idx])
        ax.set_title(f"Class {cls} – Example {col+1}")
        ax.set_xlabel("Time")
        ax.set_ylabel("Value")
        ax.set_ylim(y_min, y_max)

fig.suptitle("Three Random Training Series per Class", fontsize=14)
plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 1, 0.96])
plt.show()
```
The randomly selected training series show that both classes share a broadly similar overall shape, including gradual increases, multiple oscillations, and a sharp decline near the end of the series. Within-class variation appears relatively small, as samples from the same class exhibit similar patterns. However, the separation between classes is not clearly visible from raw time-domain plots alone, suggesting that additional analyses such as autocorrelation and frequency-domain features may be necessary for effective discrimination.

## Descriptive statistics

Compute the following for the **training set**, broken down by class:

| Statistic | Class 1 | Class 2 | … |
|-----------|---------|---------|---|
| Number of series | | | |
| Mean (across all time points and series) | | | |
| Standard deviation | | | |
| Min value | | | |
| Max value | | | |
| Series length | | | |

Report the table in your rendered HTML. Are the classes distinguishable by their
marginal statistics alone?

```{python}
stats_rows = []

for cls in np.unique(y_train):
    class_series = X_train[y_train == cls]
    class_values = class_series.flatten()

    stats_rows.append({
        "Class": cls,
        "Number of series": class_series.shape[0],
        "Mean": np.mean(class_values),
        "Standard deviation": np.std(class_values, ddof=1),
        "Min value": np.min(class_values),
        "Max value": np.max(class_values),
        "Series length": class_series.shape[1]
    })

stats_table = pd.DataFrame(stats_rows)

for col in ["Mean", "Standard deviation", "Min value", "Max value"]:
    stats_table[col] = stats_table[col].round(6)

stats_table
```
The class-wise descriptive statistics are extremely similar. Both classes contain the same number of training series and have identical series length. Their means are essentially zero, their standard deviations are the same up to the reported precision, and their minimum and maximum values are also very close. This suggests that the two classes are not distinguishable by marginal statistics alone. Any useful separation is therefore more likely to come from temporal structure, such as autocorrelation patterns or frequency-domain characteristics, rather than simple distributional summaries.

## ACF analysis

Pick **one representative series per class** (e.g. the series closest to the class mean
in Euclidean distance). Compute and plot the ACF up to lag $h = \lfloor L/4 \rfloor$,
where $L$ is the series length. Include the 95% confidence bands
$\pm 1.96/\sqrt{L}$.

1. Does either class show significant periodic structure? If so, at what lag?
2. Do the two ACF profiles differ? Describe the difference concisely.

```{python}
from statsmodels.tsa.stattools import acf

classes = np.unique(y_train)
L = X_train.shape[1]
max_lag = L // 4
conf_band = 1.96 / np.sqrt(L)

representatives = {}
rep_indices = {}

for cls in classes:
    class_series = X_train[y_train == cls]
    class_mean_series = np.mean(class_series, axis=0)

    distances = np.linalg.norm(class_series - class_mean_series, axis=1)
    best_local_idx = np.argmin(distances)
    global_idx = np.where(y_train == cls)[0][best_local_idx]

    representatives[cls] = class_series[best_local_idx]
    rep_indices[cls] = global_idx

for cls in classes:
    print(f"Class {cls}: representative series index = {rep_indices[cls]}")

fig, axes = plt.subplots(len(classes), 1, figsize=(10, 4 * len(classes)), squeeze=False)

for row, cls in enumerate(classes):
    x_rep = representatives[cls]
    acf_vals = acf(x_rep, nlags=max_lag, fft=False)
    lags = np.arange(len(acf_vals))

    ax = axes[row, 0]
    ax.stem(lags, acf_vals, basefmt=" ")
    ax.axhline(0, linestyle="--", linewidth=1)
    ax.axhline(conf_band, linestyle="--", linewidth=1)
    ax.axhline(-conf_band, linestyle="--", linewidth=1)

    ax.set_title(f"ACF of Representative Series – Class {cls} (index {rep_indices[cls]})")
    ax.set_xlabel("Lag")
    ax.set_ylabel("ACF")
    ax.set_xlim(0, max_lag)

fig.suptitle("Representative-Series ACF by Class", fontsize=14)
plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 1, 0.96])
plt.show()
```

Neither class shows a clear periodic pattern in the ACF. Instead of repeated oscillatory peaks at regular lags, both representative series display a smooth decay from strong positive autocorrelation at small lags toward negative values at medium lags. This suggests persistence in local shape rather than a stable repeating cycle. The two ACF profiles are very similar overall, indicating that the classes do not differ sharply in their basic autocorrelation structure, although Class 1 appears to decay slightly more slowly over the first several lags.

## Stationarity check

Apply the **Augmented Dickey–Fuller (ADF) test** to 5 randomly selected training
series from each class (10 series total). Report the p-values.

```
Series   Class   ADF p-value   Decision (stationary?)
-------  ------  -----------   ----------------------
  1        1
  2        1
  ...
```

*Brief interpretation: are the series (approximately) stationary? Does it matter for
the analyses that follow? (2–3 sentences)*

```{python}
from statsmodels.tsa.stattools import adfuller

classes = np.unique(y_train)
n_per_class = 5

adf_rows = []

for cls in classes:
    cls_idx = np.where(y_train == cls)[0]
    chosen_idx = np.random.choice(cls_idx, size=n_per_class, replace=False)

    for idx in chosen_idx:
        series = X_train[idx]
        adf_result = adfuller(series)
        p_value = adf_result[1]

        adf_rows.append({
            "Series": idx,
            "Class": cls,
            "ADF p-value": p_value,
            "Decision (stationary?)": "Yes" if p_value < 0.05 else "No"
        })

adf_table = pd.DataFrame(adf_rows).sort_values(["Class", "Series"]).reset_index(drop=True)
adf_table["ADF p-value"] = adf_table["ADF p-value"].round(6)

adf_table
```

The ADF test results show that all selected series have p-values greater than 0.05, meaning that the null hypothesis of a unit root cannot be rejected. This suggests that the series are not stationary. However, this is consistent with the observed smooth trends and slow decay in the ACF. While strict stationarity is not satisfied, this does not critically affect the analyses that follow, since the focus is on pattern comparison and feature extraction rather than formal time series modeling.

---

# Part 2 — Missing Data Simulation (25 pts)

Real-world time series often arrive with gaps: sensor dropouts, transmission errors,
or irregular sampling. This section asks you to simulate missing data and evaluate
imputation strategies.

## Introduce missingness

Randomly mask **10% of the time-index positions** in every training series.
Use your student ID as the random seed so your results are reproducible.

- Generate a single missing-index mask *per series* (each series gets its own
  random mask).
- Replace masked values with `NaN`.
- Do **not** modify the test set — it remains complete.

```r
# set.seed(YOUR_STUDENT_ID)
# n_missing <- floor(0.10 * L)
# For each series: sample n_missing positions without replacement → set to NA
# YOUR CODE HERE
```

```{python}
X_train_missing = X_train.copy()

L = X_train.shape[1]
n_missing = int(np.floor(0.10 * L))

missing_masks = []

for i in range(X_train.shape[0]):
    missing_idx = np.random.choice(L, size=n_missing, replace=False)
    missing_masks.append(missing_idx)
    X_train_missing[i, missing_idx] = np.nan
```

## Imputation strategies

Implement the following three strategies and apply each to **all training series**:

| Strategy | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| **Mean fill** | Replace each `NaN` with the *series mean* (computed from observed values only) |
| **Linear interpolation** | Linearly interpolate between the two nearest observed neighbours |
| **Moving-average fill** | Replace each `NaN` with the average of the nearest $w$ observed values on each side; use $w = 5$ |

Report the wall-clock time (or relative speed) of each strategy on your dataset.

```{python}
import time

# Mean fill
def mean_fill_all(X_missing):
    X_filled = X_missing.copy()

    for i in range(X_filled.shape[0]):
        series = X_filled[i]
        mean_val = np.nanmean(series)
        nan_mask = np.isnan(series)
        series[nan_mask] = mean_val
        X_filled[i] = series

    return X_filled


# Linear interpolation
def linear_interp_all(X_missing):
    X_filled = X_missing.copy()
    x = np.arange(X_filled.shape[1])

    for i in range(X_filled.shape[0]):
        series = X_filled[i]
        nan_mask = np.isnan(series)
        obs_mask = ~nan_mask

        series[nan_mask] = np.interp(x[nan_mask], x[obs_mask], series[obs_mask])
        X_filled[i] = series

    return X_filled


# Moving-average fill 
def moving_average_fill_series(series, w=5):
    filled = series.copy()
    nan_positions = np.where(np.isnan(filled))[0]

    for pos in nan_positions:
        left_vals = []
        j = pos - 1
        while j >= 0 and len(left_vals) < w:
            if not np.isnan(filled[j]):
                left_vals.append(filled[j])
            j -= 1

        right_vals = []
        j = pos + 1
        while j < len(filled) and len(right_vals) < w:
            if not np.isnan(filled[j]):
                right_vals.append(filled[j])
            j += 1

        neighbors = left_vals + right_vals

        if len(neighbors) > 0:
            filled[pos] = np.mean(neighbors)
        else:
            filled[pos] = np.nanmean(filled)

    return filled


def moving_average_fill_all(X_missing, w=5):
    X_filled = X_missing.copy()

    for i in range(X_filled.shape[0]):
        X_filled[i] = moving_average_fill_series(X_filled[i], w=w)

    return X_filled


# Timing
timing_rows = []

start = time.perf_counter()
X_train_mean = mean_fill_all(X_train_missing)
elapsed_mean = time.perf_counter() - start
timing_rows.append({"Strategy": "Mean fill", "Time (seconds)": elapsed_mean})

start = time.perf_counter()
X_train_linear = linear_interp_all(X_train_missing)
elapsed_linear = time.perf_counter() - start
timing_rows.append({"Strategy": "Linear interpolation", "Time (seconds)": elapsed_linear})

start = time.perf_counter()
X_train_ma = moving_average_fill_all(X_train_missing, w=5)
elapsed_ma = time.perf_counter() - start
timing_rows.append({"Strategy": "Moving-average fill (w=5)", "Time (seconds)": elapsed_ma})

timing_table = pd.DataFrame(timing_rows)
timing_table["Time (seconds)"] = timing_table["Time (seconds)"].round(6)
timing_table
```

## Effect on ACF

Take the **same representative series** you used in §1.3 (one per class).
For each imputation strategy, compute the ACF on the imputed series and overlay
it on the ACF of the *original* (un-masked) series.

*Deliverable: one figure per class (4 ACF curves per figure: original + 3 strategies).
One paragraph per class explaining which strategy best preserves the ACF structure
and why.*

::: {.callout-tip}
## What to look for
Mean fill inserts sharp "spikes" of constant value — this creates **artificial
autocorrelation** at all lags. Linear interpolation introduces local smoothness
that can **inflate** short-lag autocorrelation. Moving-average fill is usually
closest to the true ACF when the true series is smooth. Verify whether this
holds for your dataset.
:::

```{python}
from statsmodels.tsa.stattools import acf

L = X_train.shape[1]
max_lag = L // 4
conf_band = 1.96 / np.sqrt(L)

classes = np.unique(y_train)

for cls in classes:
    idx = rep_indices[cls]

    original_series = X_train[idx]
    mean_series = X_train_mean[idx]
    linear_series = X_train_linear[idx]
    ma_series = X_train_ma[idx]

    acf_original = acf(original_series, nlags=max_lag, fft=False)
    acf_mean = acf(mean_series, nlags=max_lag, fft=False)
    acf_linear = acf(linear_series, nlags=max_lag, fft=False)
    acf_ma = acf(ma_series, nlags=max_lag, fft=False)

    lags = np.arange(max_lag + 1)

    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 5))
    plt.plot(lags, acf_original, label="Original", linewidth=2)
    plt.plot(lags, acf_mean, label="Mean fill", linewidth=2)
    plt.plot(lags, acf_linear, label="Linear interpolation", linewidth=2)
    plt.plot(lags, acf_ma, label="Moving-average fill (w=5)", linewidth=2)

    plt.axhline(0, linestyle="--", linewidth=1)
    plt.axhline(conf_band, linestyle="--", linewidth=1)
    plt.axhline(-conf_band, linestyle="--", linewidth=1)

    plt.title(f"ACF Comparison for Representative Series – Class {cls} (index {idx})")
    plt.xlabel("Lag")
    plt.ylabel("ACF")
    plt.xlim(0, max_lag)
    plt.legend()
    plt.tight_layout()
    plt.show()
```
For Class 0, both linear interpolation and moving-average imputation closely match the original ACF, with almost indistinguishable curves across most lags. The moving-average method appears slightly smoother and marginally closer in some regions, but the difference is minimal. In contrast, mean fill deviates more clearly, especially at lower lags where it reduces autocorrelation. Overall, both linear interpolation and moving-average imputation preserve the temporal structure well, with no substantial difference between them.

A similar pattern is observed for Class 1. Linear interpolation and moving-average imputation produce ACF curves that are nearly identical to the original, indicating that both methods preserve the temporal dependence structure effectively. Any differences between these two methods are very minor and not practically significant. Mean fill again shows the largest deviation, flattening the ACF and weakening the correlation structure.

## Imputation choice

Based on the ACF comparison, which strategy would you use for downstream analysis?
Justify your choice in 3–4 sentences. Are there situations where a different choice
might be preferable?

I would choose moving-average imputation for downstream analysis. The ACF results show that mean fill distorts the dependence structure, while both linear interpolation and moving-average fill closely match the original ACF. Although the difference between these two is small, moving-average fill is slightly preferable because it better preserves local structure using nearby observed values. However, linear interpolation would also be a reasonable alternative, especially when computational simplicity and speed are important.

---

# Part 3 — Frequency Domain Analysis (25 pts)

## Periodogram

Compute the **periodogram** of 5 randomly selected training series per class using
the DFT:

$$I(\omega_j) = \frac{1}{n}\left|\sum_{t=1}^{n} x_t \, e^{-2\pi i \omega_j t}\right|^2,
\quad \omega_j = j/n, \quad j = 0, 1, \ldots, \lfloor n/2 \rfloor$$

Plot all 5 periodograms per class on the same axes (use transparency), and overlay
the **class-average periodogram** as a bold line.

```r
# Hint: use stats::spectrum() with method="pgram" and detrend=FALSE,
#        or compute manually via fft().
# YOUR CODE HERE
```

```{python}
# Hint: use scipy.signal.periodogram or numpy.fft.rfft.
classes = np.unique(y_train)
L = X_train.shape[1]
n_per_class = 5

selected_periodogram_indices = {}

fig, axes = plt.subplots(len(classes), 1, figsize=(10, 5 * len(classes)), squeeze=False)

for row, cls in enumerate(classes):
    cls_idx = np.where(y_train == cls)[0]
    chosen_idx = np.random.choice(cls_idx, size=n_per_class, replace=False)
    selected_periodogram_indices[cls] = chosen_idx

    freqs = np.arange(0, L // 2 + 1) / L
    class_periodograms = []

    ax = axes[row, 0]

    for idx in chosen_idx:
        series = X_train[idx]

        fft_vals = np.fft.rfft(series)
        pxx = (1 / L) * np.abs(fft_vals) ** 2

        class_periodograms.append(pxx)

        ax.plot(freqs, pxx, alpha=0.35, linewidth=1)

    class_periodograms = np.array(class_periodograms)
    avg_periodogram = class_periodograms.mean(axis=0)

    ax.plot(freqs, avg_periodogram, linewidth=2.5, label="Class-average periodogram")

    ax.set_title(f"Periodograms for 5 Random Training Series – Class {cls}")
    ax.set_xlabel("Frequency")
    ax.set_ylabel("Power")
    ax.legend()

fig.suptitle("Class-wise Periodogram Comparison", fontsize=14)
plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 1, 0.97])
plt.show()

for cls in classes:
    print(f"Class {cls}: selected indices = {selected_periodogram_indices[cls]}")

```

## Dominant frequency

For each class, identify the **dominant frequency** $\omega^*$ — the frequency at
which the average periodogram attains its maximum (excluding $\omega = 0$).

Report:

| Class | Dominant frequency $\omega^*$ | Dominant period $1/\omega^*$ | Peak power |
|-------|-------------------------------|------------------------------|------------|
| 1 | | | |
| 2 | | | |

Does the dominant frequency differ between classes? Could $\omega^*$ alone
discriminate between them?

```{python}
dominant_rows = []

for cls in classes:
    chosen_idx = selected_periodogram_indices[cls]

    freqs = np.arange(0, L // 2 + 1) / L
    class_periodograms = []

    for idx in chosen_idx:
        series = X_train[idx]
        fft_vals = np.fft.rfft(series)
        pxx = (1 / L) * np.abs(fft_vals) ** 2
        class_periodograms.append(pxx)

    class_periodograms = np.array(class_periodograms)
    avg_periodogram = class_periodograms.mean(axis=0)

    # Exclude omega = 0
    freqs_nozero = freqs[1:]
    avg_nozero = avg_periodogram[1:]

    dom_idx = np.argmax(avg_nozero)
    omega_star = freqs_nozero[dom_idx]
    peak_power = avg_nozero[dom_idx]
    dominant_period = 1 / omega_star

    dominant_rows.append({
        "Class": cls,
        "Dominant frequency ω*": omega_star,
        "Dominant period 1/ω*": dominant_period,
        "Peak power": peak_power
    })

dominant_table = pd.DataFrame(dominant_rows)

for col in ["Dominant frequency ω*", "Dominant period 1/ω*", "Peak power"]:
    dominant_table[col] = dominant_table[col].round(6)

dominant_table
```

The dominant frequency is identical for both classes, with ω* ≈ 0.003497, corresponding to a period of approximately 286, which matches the full series length. This suggests that the dominant component reflects a global trend or low-frequency variation rather than a distinct periodic behavior. The peak power values are also very similar, indicating no meaningful difference in spectral dominance between classes. As a result, the dominant frequency ω* alone is not sufficient to discriminate between the classes.

## Smoothed vs. raw periodogram

For one representative series per class, compare:
- the **raw periodogram** (high variance, inconsistent)
- a **smoothed periodogram** with bandwidth $L = 9$ (average 9 adjacent
  frequency bins using a Daniell/boxcar kernel)

Plot both on the same axes. Annotate the dominant peak with its frequency and
corresponding period.

*In 2–3 sentences: what is gained (and what is lost) by smoothing?*

```{python}
bandwidth = 9
half_window = bandwidth // 2

classes = np.unique(y_train)
L = X_train.shape[1]
freqs = np.arange(0, L // 2 + 1) / L

fig, axes = plt.subplots(len(classes), 1, figsize=(10, 5 * len(classes)), squeeze=False)

for row, cls in enumerate(classes):
    idx = rep_indices[cls]
    series = X_train[idx]

    fft_vals = np.fft.rfft(series)
    raw_pxx = (1 / L) * np.abs(fft_vals) ** 2

    kernel = np.ones(bandwidth) / bandwidth
    smooth_pxx = np.convolve(raw_pxx, kernel, mode="same")

    dom_idx_raw = np.argmax(raw_pxx[1:]) + 1
    dom_freq_raw = freqs[dom_idx_raw]
    dom_period_raw = 1 / dom_freq_raw

    dom_idx_smooth = np.argmax(smooth_pxx[1:]) + 1
    dom_freq_smooth = freqs[dom_idx_smooth]
    dom_period_smooth = 1 / dom_freq_smooth

    ax = axes[row, 0]
    ax.plot(freqs, raw_pxx, label="Raw periodogram", alpha=0.7, linewidth=1.5)
    ax.plot(freqs, smooth_pxx, label="Smoothed periodogram (L=9)", linewidth=2.5)

    ax.scatter(dom_freq_smooth, smooth_pxx[dom_idx_smooth], zorder=5)
    ax.annotate(
        f"Peak: ω={dom_freq_smooth:.4f}\nPeriod={dom_period_smooth:.1f}",
        xy=(dom_freq_smooth, smooth_pxx[dom_idx_smooth]),
        xytext=(dom_freq_smooth + 0.03, smooth_pxx[dom_idx_smooth] * 0.85),
        arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="->", lw=1),
        fontsize=9
    )

    ax.set_title(f"Raw vs Smoothed Periodogram – Class {cls} (index {idx})")
    ax.set_xlabel("Frequency")
    ax.set_ylabel("Power")
    ax.legend()

fig.suptitle("Raw and Smoothed Periodograms for Representative Series", fontsize=14)
plt.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 1, 0.97])
plt.show()
```

Smoothing significantly reduces the high variance of the raw periodogram, making the dominant frequency much easier to identify. While the raw periodogram contains many sharp and noisy spikes, the smoothed version reveals a clear and stable peak around ω ≈ 0.0175 (period ≈ 57). This improves interpretability but comes at the cost of reduced spectral resolution, as finer frequency details and narrow peaks are smoothed out.

## Time domain vs. frequency domain

Does the dominant period you found in §3.2 correspond to any periodic structure
you observed in the ACF (§1.3)?
If yes: at which ACF lag does the peak appear, and does it match $1/\omega^*$?
If no: speculate why the two analyses might disagree for your dataset.

No, the dominant period from §3.2 is not matched by any clear periodic peak in the ACF from §1.3. Instead of repeated local maxima at regular lags, the ACF showed a gradual decay and then moderate negative autocorrelation, which is more consistent with smooth non-stationary structure than with a stable cycle. This suggests that the dominant frequency in the class-average periodogram mainly captures a very low-frequency global trend, with period approximately equal to the series length, rather than a meaningful repeating pattern. Therefore, the two analyses disagree because they are emphasizing different aspects of the data: the periodogram highlights low-frequency spectral power, while the ACF reflects overall persistence and trend-like behavior.

---

# Part 4 — Robustness Analysis (15 pts)

A key practical question: *how much noise can a spectral analysis tolerate before the
dominant frequency becomes undetectable?*

## Noise injection

Take **10 randomly selected training series** (balanced across classes if possible).
For each series $x_t$ of length $L$ with standard deviation $\sigma_x$, create two
noisy versions:

$$x_t^{(1)} = x_t + \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\,(0.2\sigma_x)^2)$$
$$x_t^{(2)} = x_t + \varepsilon_t, \quad \varepsilon_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\,(0.5\sigma_x)^2)$$

Compute the periodogram of the original and both noisy versions.

## Peak degradation

For each of the 10 series, record the dominant frequency $\omega^*$ and the
**signal-to-noise ratio at the peak** (ratio of peak power to the median power
across all frequencies) for the original and both noise levels:

| Series | $\omega^*$ (orig.) | SNR (orig.) | $\omega^*$ (low noise) | SNR (low) | $\omega^*$ (high noise) | SNR (high) |
|--------|--------------------|-------------|------------------------|-----------|------------------------|------------|
| 1 | | | | | | |
| … | | | | | | |

*Does the dominant frequency $\omega^*$ shift under noise, or does the peak simply
become less prominent?*

## Detectability threshold

Considering the 10 series together: at what noise level (if any) does the dominant
spectral peak become **indistinguishable from noise** (i.e. SNR ≤ 3 dB)?
Does this threshold depend on the class of the series?

*Deliverable: one paragraph + supporting figure (e.g. box plot of SNR across 10
series, by noise level).*

## Implication for analysis

In 3–4 sentences: what does this robustness experiment tell you about the
practical reliability of spectral features for classifying your dataset?
Under what conditions would frequency-domain analysis fail as a discriminator?

---

# Part 5 — Held-out Prediction (10 pts)

This section asks you to build the simplest possible classifier from the spectral
features you computed and evaluate it on the **test set** (which was not used
in any prior analysis).

## Feature extraction

For each **training** series, compute the following features from the *original*
(un-masked) series:

1. Dominant frequency $\omega^*$ (location of the periodogram maximum)
2. Peak power (value of periodogram at $\omega^*$)
3. Fraction of total power in the top-3 frequencies (spectral concentration)
4. Mean ACF at lags 1–5 (average of $|\hat\gamma(1)|, \ldots, |\hat\gamma(5)|$)

Assemble these into a feature matrix $\mathbf{F}_\text{train} \in \mathbb{R}^{n_\text{train} \times 4}$.
Repeat for the test set to get $\mathbf{F}_\text{test}$.

## Nearest-centroid classifier

For each class, compute the **centroid** (mean feature vector) from training
examples. Assign each test series to the class whose centroid is closest in
Euclidean distance.

```
accuracy = (number of correctly classified test series) / (total test series)
```

Report accuracy and compare to the **majority-class baseline**
(always predict the most frequent training class).

## Reflection

Did spectral features alone provide useful discrimination?
Compare your result to the UCR archive's reported accuracy for your dataset
(listed on the archive webpage). What does the gap suggest about what information
your 4 features are missing?

---

# Part 6 — Decision Log (required, 300–400 words) {#sec-log}

This section is graded on **specificity and honesty**, not on whether your choices
were optimal. Generic statements ("I chose linear interpolation because it is commonly
used") receive no credit. Statements referencing your specific dataset and results
("For dataset X, mean fill created a spurious ACF peak at lag 12 that was not present
in the original; linear interpolation preserved the ACF structure up to lag 20") receive
full credit.

Address the following four prompts:

1. **Two key decisions** you made during the analysis (e.g. choice of imputation
   strategy, bandwidth for smoothing, how you handled edge cases) — explain *why*
   you made each decision based on evidence from your own data.

2. **One surprising finding** that you did not expect before starting the analysis.
   What does it reveal about your dataset?

3. **One limitation** of the analysis pipeline in this homework. What would you
   do differently with more time?

4. **One open question** your analysis raised that this homework did not answer.

---

*IE 48B — Special Topics in Time Series Analytics, Spring 2026*
*Mustafa Gökçe Baydoğan*
